notes: Run 2026-07-01. 15 clues across: American Literature (1), Authors & Works (2), Awards/Movements/Terms (2), British & Irish Literature (3), Children's & YA (1), Literary Characters (1), Poetry (1), Shakespeare & Drama (2), World Literature (2). Tier: $200×1, $400×4, $600×6, $800×2, $1000×2. $1000 tier leaned in to address under-weight gap (The Pickwick Papers, George Eliot).

questions.clue: This 1908 L. M. Montgomery novel follows a red-haired 11-year-old orphan mistakenly sent to a Prince Edward Island farm whose guardians had intended to adopt a boy to help with the work.

citations.title: Anne of Green Gables
citations.snippet: Anne of Green Gables is a 1908 novel by the Canadian author Lucy Maud Montgomery. Written for all ages, it has been considered a classic children's novel since the mid-20th century. Set in the late 19th century, the novel recounts the adven

questions.clue: This 1943 Betty Smith novel about a girl coming of age in a New York City tenement was so popular it went to a second printing before its official publication date and was later named by the Library of Congress as one of the 'Books That Shaped America.'

citations.title: A Tree Grows in Brooklyn (novel)
citations.snippet: A Tree Grows in Brooklyn is a 1943 semi-autobiographical novel written by Betty Smith.

questions.clue: Robert Louis Stevenson's 1886 Gothic horror novella follows a London lawyer named Utterson who investigates the disturbing connection between his respectable old friend and the violent criminal who shadows him through the city at night.
questions.answer: Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde
aliases: The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde

citations.title: Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde
citations.snippet: Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde is an 1886 Gothic horror novella by Scottish author Robert Louis Stevenson. It follows Gabriel John Utterson, a London-based legal practitioner who investigates a series of strange occurrences between h

questions.clue: Arthur Miller's 1949 Broadway tragedy follows Willy Loman, a middle-aged traveling businessman slipping into senility who confronts the chasm between his failed life and the version of the American Dream he had always imagined for himself.

citations.title: Death of a Salesman
citations.snippet: Death of a Salesman is a 1949 stage play written by the American playwright Arthur Miller. The play premiered on Broadway on February 10, 1949, running for 742 performances. It is a two-act tragedy—the subtitle of the play is "Certain Priva

questions.clue: Written by Edward Lear for a three-year-old and first published in 1870, this beloved nonsense poem introduced the phrase 'runcible spoon' and follows two creatures who sail away in a pea-green boat and marry by the light of the moon.

citations.title: The Owl and the Pussy-Cat
citations.snippet: "The Owl and the Pussy-Cat" is a nonsense poem by Edward Lear, first published in 1870 in the American magazine Our Young Folks and again the following year in Lear's own book Nonsense Songs, Stories, Botany, and Alphabets. Lear wrote the p

questions.clue: All six major novels by this English author were originally published anonymously, their title pages identifying her only as 'By a Lady'; her sharp social commentary and irony eventually earned her a place among the most widely read writers in English literature.

citations.title: Jane Austen
citations.snippet: Jane Austen was an English writer known primarily for her six novels, which implicitly interpret, critique, and comment on the English landed gentry at the end of the 18th century.

questions.clue: Born François-Marie Arouet, this French Enlightenment writer took a pen name by which the world knows him; he is celebrated for his sharp criticism of the Catholic Church, his advocacy for freedom of speech, and his extraordinary output of more than 2,000 books and pamphlets.

citations.title: Voltaire
citations.snippet: François-Marie Arouet, known by his pen name Voltaire, was a French Enlightenment writer, philosopher (philosophe), satirist, and historian. Famous for his wit and his criticism of Christianity and of slavery, Voltaire was an advocate of fr

questions.clue: This Russian playwright and short-story writer, who also practiced as a physician, described his dual vocation by saying, 'Medicine is my lawful wife, and literature is my mistress.'

citations.title: Anton Chekhov
citations.snippet: Anton Pavlovich Chekhov was a Russian playwright and short story writer. Widely considered one of the greatest writers of all time, his career as a playwright produced four classics, and his best short stories are held in high esteem by wri

questions.clue: Known to posterity by a single name, this medieval Italian poet chose to compose his major works in the Tuscan vernacular at a time when Latin dominated scholarly writing, a decision that helped establish the Florentine dialect as the literary language of Italy.

citations.title: Dante Alighieri
citations.snippet: Dante Alighieri, widely known mononymously as Dante, was an Italian poet, writer, and philosopher. His Divine Comedy, originally called Comedìa and later christened Divina by Giovanni Boccaccio, is widely considered one of the most importan

questions.clue: Acclaimed as 'the greatest prose writer of Russian Jewry' and best known for his Odessa Stories and Red Cavalry collection, this Soviet author was arrested on fabricated espionage charges by the NKVD and executed in January 1940.

citations.title: Isaac Babel
citations.snippet: Isaac Emmanuilovich Babel was a Russian and Soviet writer, journalist, playwright, and literary translator. He is best known as the author of Red Cavalry and Odessa Stories, and has been acclaimed as "the greatest prose writer of Russian Je

questions.clue: Salman Rushdie's 1981 Booker Prize-winning novel interweaves India's transition from colonial rule to independence with the magical-realist story of Saleem Sinai, born at the precise moment of that independence.

citations.title: Midnight's Children
citations.snippet: Midnight's Children is the second novel by Indian-British writer Salman Rushdie, published in 1981 by Jonathan Cape with cover design by Bill Botten, about India's transition from British colonial rule to independence and partition. It is a

questions.clue: In 2014, this Norwegian playwright was ranked as the world's most frequently performed dramatist after Shakespeare, a testament to his lasting influence as the pioneering figure of theatrical realism often called 'the father of modern drama.'

citations.title: Henrik Ibsen
citations.snippet: Henrik Johan Ibsen was a Norwegian playwright. He is considered one of the world's pre-eminent writers of the 19th century and is often referred to as "the father of modern drama". He pioneered theatrical realism but also wrote lyrical epic

questions.clue: In Charlotte Brontë's 1847 novel, this madwoman — the first wife of Thornfield Hall's brooding master — is confined to a hidden third-floor room, her existence concealed from the young governess who has come to love him.

citations.title: Bertha Mason
citations.snippet: Bertha Antoinetta Rochester is a character in Charlotte Brontë's 1847 novel Jane Eyre. She is described as the violently insane first wife of Edward Rochester, who moved her to Thornfield Hall and locked her in a room on the third floor.

questions.clue: Originally published in monthly installments from March 1836 to November 1837, this English comic novel began as prose descriptions commissioned to accompany a set of 'cockney sporting plates' by illustrator Robert Seymour.
aliases: Posthumous Papers of the Pickwick Club

citations.title: The Pickwick Papers
citations.snippet: The Posthumous Papers of the Pickwick Club was the first novel by English author Charles Dickens, first published in serial form from March 1836 to November 1837. Because of his success with Sketches by Boz published in 1836, Dickens was as

questions.clue: This Victorian novelist's final work, published in 1876, interweaves the story of the spirited Gwendolen Harleth with that of a young man awakening to his Jewish heritage — an ambitious departure from the English provincial life her earlier novels charted.

citations.title: Daniel Deronda
citations.snippet: Daniel Deronda is a novel by English author George Eliot, first published in eight installments from February to September 1876. It was the last novel she completed and the only one set in the Victorian society of her day. The work's mixtur
